nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs, lagoons, estuaries, and soft/muddy bottoms in tropical and subtropical waters; commonly observed sheltering in caves and ledges by day.

decorPreferences: Live rock with caves and ledges for daytime refuge
Open swimming space for active mid-water cruising

diet.notes: Meaty foods are the staple — krill, squid, clams, mussel, prawns, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey is essential because the fused beak grows continuously and must be worn down to prevent overgrowth; offer whole shell-on shrimp, snails, and crab pieces several times per week. Feed small amounts 2-3 times daily for juveniles, less often for adults. Avoid floating foods to discourage air-gulping, which can cause buoyancy problems.

goodWith: Tangs and surgeonfish of similar size
avoidWith: All ornamental invertebrates (shrimp, crabs, snails, starfish)
Corals and clams (inverts will be eaten or nipped)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 7683x5122. Sharp full lateral profile, large blue eye, tan/beige body with dark spots and brown blotches, erect white spines and pale belly — all diagnostic features clearly shown.
Brocken Inaglory; 1620x1106. Full-body in-situ from a dorsal-lateral angle showing the mottled brown blotches and dark spots over a pale body; good variety versus the profile shot.
User:Strobilomyces; 1728x1152. Swimming individual with long spines fully erect, showing the iconic porcupinefish look and big eye; sharp and well-lit.

summary: The Porcupine Puffer is a large, personable saltwater fish widely noted for recognising its keeper and begging at the glass. Not reef-safe, it belongs in a spacious fish-only system where hard-shelled foods help keep its continuously growing beak worn down.
careNotes: The Porcupine Puffer is widely regarded as one of the more personable saltwater fish in the hobby, with many keepers reporting that individuals quickly learn to recognise the person who feeds them and actively beg at the glass. It is broadly considered not reef-safe — it readily crunches shrimp, crabs, snails, and urchins, and tends to sample corals and clams as well — making a fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) setup the appropriate choice. The fused beak (four teeth fused into a parrot-like crushing plate) grows continuously throughout the fish's life and must be worn down by regularly offering hard-shelled foods — whole shell-on shrimp, mussels in shell, crab pieces, and snails — several times per week; an overgrown beak can prevent feeding and is difficult to correct. Adults are generally reported to reach around 30 cm in aquaria, though the species can attain 50 cm total length in the wild, so a tank with a long footprint (around 180 cm and at least 700 L) is generally advised for a full-grown specimen. The Porcupine Puffer produces considerable waste, so an oversized protein skimmer and weekly water changes of around 20% are broadly recommended. Lighting should be increased gradually, as the species tends to startle easily. When threatened or stressed, Porcupine Puffers inflate by swallowing water; chronic stress is harmful, so deliberately triggering this response should be avoided. Like all members of the family Diodontidae, the skin and viscera contain tetrodotoxin — a potent toxin that is primarily a hazard for human consumption, but stressed or dying individuals may release it into a closed system, which can harm or kill tankmates.
breedingNotes: Not commercially bred in captivity. Spawning has been observed in the wild (pelagic eggs scattered in open water) but is not documented in home aquaria. Sexing is not reliably possible by external morphology.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Provided care level, water parameters (SG 1.020-1.025, 70-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12), 180 gal minimum tank, and beak-wear feeding guidance. Original prod/859 URL 404'd; located current URL via search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Diodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), confirmed circumtropical range, depth 2-200 m (typically 2-35 m), max length 50 cm TL, temperature 21.5-29°C, nocturnal carnivore on molluscs/urchins/crustaceans.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Diodon holocanthus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy, distribution (Florida/Bahamas to Brazil; Japan to Lord Howe Island), parrot-like crushing beak, omnivorous nocturnal feeder on hard-shelled prey, 50 cm max length, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: FishKeeper UK
additional.notes: Adult size 30 cm, temperature 23-27°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025; recommends 3 daily feedings, hard-shelled foods for beak maintenance, gradual lighting transitions.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented in home aquaria. In the wild, Porcupine Puffers are pelagic spawners, releasing eggs into open water.
